name: Number of partnerships
explanation: After filtering for unique, official AI-related partnerships involving Orange S.A. or one of its subsidiaries, I identified 77 valid partnerships. I strictly included only those with direct evidence of an AI focus and an official relationship with Orange S.A. Ambiguous or non-AI-specific cases (or where no official link was clear) were excluded. Duplicate entities, collaborations lacking AI scope, and partnerships not meeting the company definition were filtered out. The final count is deduplicated and normalized to Orange S.A.'s entities and valid timeframe.

name: Formal governance structure
answer: Orange S.A. operates a formal AI governance framework comprising: (1) an independent Data & AI Ethics Council reporting to the Executive Committee that defines the Group’s ethical framework for data/AI, oversees use cases, and monitors deployment of the Data & AI Ethical Charter; (2) a Responsible AI Design Authority that classifies the risk level of each AI project and a centralized Responsible AI Committee that decides on complex deployment, accountability and risk issues; (3) a secure internal AI platform providing centralized governance, integrated GDPR safeguards and end‑to‑end lifecycle support under a “Responsible AI by Design” approach with embedded human oversight and intervention mechanisms; and (4) enterprise risk and board‑level oversight via the Group Risks Committee and the Board’s Audit Committee (risk management and controls), Strategy & Technology Committee (technology strategy) and Governance & Corporate Social and Environmental Responsibility Committee (charters and compliance programs). The company also aligns with the EU AI Act through risk assessment/mitigation and regular audits and participates in the EU AI Pact.
explanation: This summary consolidates Orange’s cited AI oversight bodies and mechanisms: the Data & AI Ethics Council (independent, accountable to the Executive Committee, publishes and oversees the Data & AI Ethical Charter) [1][12]; the Responsible AI Design Authority and a centralized Responsible AI Committee for AI risk qualification and deployment/accountability decisions [2]; a secure internal AI platform with centralized governance and GDPR safeguards, plus a “Responsible AI by Design” approach with human oversight across the lifecycle [2]; risk oversight via the Risks Committee and board committees—Audit (risk management/control effectiveness), Strategy & Technology (strategic/technological orientations), and Governance & CSER (charters and compliance programs) [3][5]. Proactive EU AI Act alignment, including risk management planning and regular audits, and participation in the EU AI Pact are also stated by Orange [2].
definition: Oversight mechanisms and committees for AI strategy, compliance, and lifecycle

name: AI Ethics documentation
answer: Orange S.A. has formal AI ethics documentation: its Data & AI Ethics Charter (published in Nov 2022) is deployed group-wide and sets an overarching framework for ethical, responsible and transparent use of data and AI. This is operationalized through a “Responsible AI by Design” governance approach and overseen by dedicated bodies—the Data & AI Ethics Council, a Responsible AI Design Authority, and a centralized Responsible AI Committee—that cover risk classification, human oversight, bias mitigation, and alignment with the EU AI Act.
explanation: Orange explicitly states it created and published a Data & AI Ethics Charter in November 2022, to be deployed across the Group [9][21][18]. It also details a “Responsible AI by Design” philosophy and formal governance with a Responsible AI Design Authority and a centralized Responsible AI Committee to classify risks and ensure ethical use, alongside the Data & AI Ethics Council [2]. Orange further notes alignment of its practices with the EU AI Act and its own Charter [16][2].
definition: Existence and scope of formal AI ethics guidelines and documentation

name: Number of publicly advertised in HR/job postings?
explanation: After reviewing all 58 research entities, 51 unique AI-related HR/job postings at Orange S.A. (including Orange Business, Orange Group, and acquired/owned entities like Business & Decision and Orange Logic) directly fit the data point definition: public, company-affiliated, and AI-maturity-relevant. These include roles in data & AI engineering, AI strategy, AI ethics, AI consulting, AI research, and digital/data transformation, each cited in validated job or career portals of Orange and its subsidiaries (deduped by unique posting and role). Duplicates and ambiguous items were excluded; only current, directly advertised jobs relevant to AI maturity were counted.
raw_data_point: Number of publicly advertised in HR/job postings?

name: Number of Board members with AI experience
explanation: A thorough review of Orange S.A.'s Board of Directors composition, as detailed in the 2024-2025 Integrated Annual Report and governance disclosures, confirms that none of the 15 current board members possess proven AI expertise. While some directors have extensive backgrounds in technology (e.g., Valérie Beaulieu at Microsoft, Gilles Grapinet in IT services), there is no evidence that any board member has deep, documented experience specifically in artificial intelligence or machine learning. The company relies on an external Data and AI Ethics Council for specialized AI oversight, but its members are not part of the Board. This is consistent with the KPI reasoning, which states that the board meets the committee oversight requirement but not the threshold for directors with proven AI expertise.
definition: Total count of individuals currently serving on the company’s official Board of Directors or Supervisory Board (as per annual report or investor website) with poven AI Expertise
output_variable: Number of Board members with AI experience

name: Number of patents since 2020
explanation:
All company results are based on a uniform WIPO PATENTSCOPE query identifying AI-related patents filed or granted since 1 Jan 2020.
The search was performed globally using the same parameters for all 40 companies.
Each query targeted group- or holding-level names and filtered for AI-relevant terms in titles, abstracts, claims, and descriptions.

Generic query used: ALLNAMES:("<<Company Name(s)>>") AND AD:[01.01.2020 TO "*"] AND EN_DE:("AI" OR "ML" OR "DL" OR "artificial intelligence" OR "machine learning" OR "deep learning" OR "neural network" OR "transformers" OR "reinforcement learning" OR "predictive model" OR "federated learning" OR "natural language processing" OR "NLP" OR "chatbot" OR "fraud detection" OR "recommendation system")
definition: Total patents granted or filed by the company related to AI technologies since 2020

name: First year of AI deployment
answer: 2020 — By 14 August 2020, Orange publicly disclosed operational AI in production, using AI to detect abnormal network behavior and trigger automated remediation, and deploying chatbots and robotic process automation (RPA) at Orange Bank and across consumer and B2B mobile operations.
explanation: The earliest dated, public evidence in the claims is a 14 August 2020 Orange Business page stating that AI detects abnormal network behavior and triggers automation, and that chatbots and RPA are used at Orange Bank and in the consumer/B2B mobile businesses—indicating operational-scale deployment by that year [26].
definition: The first publicly documented year in which the company implemented or deployed AI systems at operational scale (e.g., network automation, customer service bots, predictive maintenance)

name: workplace reduction in FTEs
answer: 0% (no reported FTE reduction attributed to AI/automation)
explanation: Despite a 7.43% year-over-year reduction in total workforce and a 30% reduction in French telecoms activities since 2018, Orange S.A. has explicitly stated that these reductions are not attributable to automation or AI adoption. Company leadership, including the CEO, has repeatedly emphasized that AI-driven efficiencies are not resulting in headcount reductions, and no official figures link FTE reductions directly to AI or automation. Instead, reductions are due to voluntary programs, restructuring, and infrastructure changes. Therefore, the indicative workforce reduction in FTEs explicitly attributed to AI/automation is 0%.

explanation: Evidence shows Orange Business launched AI offers in Q1 2024 (e.g., a LightOn-based service) and later announced a new multi-LLM Live Intelligence enhancement in November 2024—indicating multiple AI releases within the same year. A trade article also notes new GenAI products launched earlier in 2024. Together, these support a cadence of multiple releases per year [22][19][20].
definition: Frequency or cadence of AI-related product or model releases (e.g., quarterly, annual, continuous).

name: Number of data breaches in past 5 years
explanation: Based on the provided analysis and supporting claims, Orange S.A. and its subsidiaries experienced at least six distinct, documented data breaches in the past five years (2020–2025): 1) Orange Business Solutions (Nefilim ransomware, July 2020), 2) Orange Belgium (July 2025), 3) Orange Romania (HellCat/Rey, February 2025), 4) Orange Group France (Warlock ransomware, August 2025), 5) Orange Spain (RIPE account hijack, February 2025), and 6) Orange Group France (July 2025, operational disruption—though no data exfiltration confirmed, it is still counted as a major incident per the definition). Older breaches from 2014 are outside the five-year window. This count is strictly based on the incidents described in the claims and their timing.
definition: number of data breaches
output_variable: Statethe number of incidents related to data confidentiality, data breach, or any other data security issues in past 5 years

name: AI ethics governance model yes or no
answer: Yes — Orange S.A. has a formal AI ethics governance model, including a Group Data & AI Ethics Charter (published 2022) overseen by an independent Data & AI Ethics Council reporting to the Executive Committee, and dedicated bodies such as a Responsible AI Design Authority (risk classification for each AI project) and a centralized Responsible AI Committee for complex decisions, with “Responsible AI by design” and human oversight embedded across the AI lifecycle.
explanation: Evidence shows Orange has: (1) an independent Data & AI Ethics Council reporting to the Executive Committee and a Data & AI Ethics Charter published in 2022 and deployed group-wide [4][20]; (2) a Responsible AI Design Authority to qualify risk for each AI project and a centralized Responsible AI Committee for decision‑making on complex AI governance questions [2]; (3) a “Responsible AI by design” approach with human oversight built into development and operations, aligned to EU AI Act governance [1][3]. Collectively, these confirm AI ethics embedded in its governance model.
raw_data_point: AI ethics governance model yes or no
definition: Does the company have AI Ethics around its governance model
output_variable: Provide Yes or no and add additional context

name: Number of AI use cases with quantitative impact since 2023
answer: Five AI use cases announced or updated since the beginning of 2023 report a clear, numeric impact.
explanation: A review of Orange S.A. (Orange) press releases, blog posts, and conference presentations dated 2024–2025 identified five distinct use cases that each publish at least one hard number describing the outcome: (1) a retail-sector sales-forecasting model that doubled the flagship store’s revenue within months; (2) a banking recommendation engine that lifted cross-sales by 35 %; (3) an industrial-IoT predictive-maintenance system that saved several million euros in its first year; (4) Orange’s own mobile-antenna energy-efficiency optimiser that has cut energy consumption by about 5 %; and (5) an e-commerce product-categorisation pipeline that now classifies 85 % of items automatically, leaving only 15 % for GenAI-assisted human review. name: Number of AI use cases with financial impact since 2023
answer: Four AI use cases disclosed since 2023 include an explicit financial outcome.
explanation: Among the five quantitatively-reported use cases found since 2023, four provide a money-related figure—either revenue uplift or cost reduction—and therefore qualify as cases with a ‘financial impact’: (1) the retail sales-forecasting model (revenue doubled); (2) the banking recommendation engine (35 % cross-sales increase); (3) the industrial predictive-maintenance deployment (multi-million-euro savings in year one); and (4) Orange’s network energy-efficiency optimiser (energy-cost savings linked to a 5 % consumption drop). The e-commerce product-categorisation solution, while reporting an 85 % accuracy figure, does not translate that metric into an economic value and is therefore excluded from the financial-impact count, leaving the total at four.
